column1: # Principle: Number arithmetic — Integer + Decimal + operator dispatch.
# This file is the canonical numeric-operations spec. It covers:
#   - The two equivalence classes for non-commutative arrangements
#   - Composition with custom user-defined functions (def + fn)
#   - Type-mismatch errors and IEEE-754 float artefacts
# The three test words exercised here are representative of every
# numeric op pattern. mulq is registered for use in other spec files
# but its behaviour is identical to addq's (commutative binary,
# [TNumber, TNumber], decimal contagion). Other production words like
# div, pow, abs are not in the spec runner's word set.
# AQL has two concrete numeric types under the same Number root:
#   Scalar/Number/Integer  — written as digits with optional leading minus
#   Scalar/Number/Decimal  — written with a decimal point
# All three test words are sigged on [TNumber, TNumber] (or [TNumber|]
# for unary — the `|` makes the arg forward-eligible with stack
# fallback). Integer and Decimal both match Number. Each handler
# dispatches on argument types at runtime:
#     - both args Integer  →  integer op,  result is Integer
#     - any arg Decimal    →  float64 op,  result is Decimal  (contagion)
# All non-commutative binary handlers compute `result = b OP a` where
# `a = args[0]`, `b = args[1]`. Combined with the matching algorithm
# (forward args fill sig[0..F-1] in source order, then stack args fill
# sig[F..N-1] top-down), the natural infix reading just works:
# The same algorithm makes `subq 3 10` and `10 3 subq` produce the
# same result (sig=[3, 10] in all three cases). The other three
# arrangements (`subq 10 3`, `3 subq 10`, `3 10 subq`) form the second
# equivalence class with sig=[10, 3] and return -7. There is NO "swap
# form" — every arrangement is equally valid; the matching algorithm
# just maps each one to a specific (args[0], args[1]) pair. See
# Numeric dispatch is the foundational test of the type lattice and
# the matching algorithm together. The Integer/Decimal split is the
# language's first lattice decision; the contagion rule is its first
# closure rule; the b-op-a handler convention is the production
# engine's first surface contract. These rows pin all three.
# Integer (already covered in literals.tsv but repeated here for the
# Decimal — formatDecimal always emits ".0" or an exponent so the
# Decimal type tag is visually distinct from Integer.

column3: Integer + Decimal coexist; type tag visible per-value

column1: # ---------- 2. addq — commutative binary across all I/D combinations ----------
# Three structural arrangements (prefix, infix, postfix) × four type
# combinations (II, ID, DI, DD). addq is commutative so the value is
# invariant under sig flip; we verify the type-promotion rule.

column3: stack=[2, 3.0]: top=3.0 → sig[0], deeper=2 → sig[1]; result is 5.0 (Decimal contagion)

column1: # Specific decimal values to surface arithmetic correctness:

column3: non-integer fractions sum to a whole — but result still renders as Decimal "4.0"

column1: # ---------- 3. subq — non-commutative binary, both equivalence classes ----------
# Class A — the matching algorithm produces sig=[3, 10] (or sig=[X, Y]
# for X=3, Y=10), so handler returns b - a = 10 - 3 = 7.
# Class B — sig=[10, 3] (or sig=[Y, X]), handler returns 3 - 10 = -7.
# A specific decimal-only subtraction surfaces a float artefact.

column3: infix form of the same — sig=[0.9, 1.0] → 1.0-0.9

column1: # Sig [Number|] — barrier at position 1 (= N for unary). The single
# arg is forward-eligible with stack fallback, so both forms work:
#   X negq   — fwd-scan empty, stack supplies the arg
# Type-preserving: Integer in, Integer out; Decimal in, Decimal out.

column3: Integer zero stays zero (no sign bit)
Decimal zero → -0.0 (IEEE sign flip; +0.0 and -0.0 are distinct bit patterns)

column1: # IEEE 754 binary float cannot represent 0.1 or 0.2 exactly. Their
# sum is 0.30000000000000004, never 0.3. aqleng preserves this
# verbatim — no display rounding, no silent truncation. See
# SPEC_REPORT.md §2 for the exact-decimal-port plan that would
# Parens force the inner expression to evaluate before the outer's
# slot is filled. This is also the workaround for nested forward
# (see nested-forward.tsv: function words are structural boundaries
# in matchSignature; bare nesting `addq 1 mulq 2 3` errors).

column3: inner subq class B: fwd=[1, 5] → sig=[1, 5] → 5-1 = 4; outer 2.0 * 4 → 8.0 (contagion)
composes via paren: inner addq = 5; negq(5) = -5
inner subq is class B → 3-10 = -7; outer negq(-7) = 7

column1: # Parens leaving multivalues — both flow into outer arg slots.
# `end` short-circuits forward collection for the nearest pending
# word. After `end`, forward collection stops and the engine
# resolves the call from whatever was already collected (plus stack

column3: end after addq is fully resolved is harmless — addq(3,2) already fired
end forces close with fwd=[2] only; stack empty so sig[1] can't be filled
same shape — end forces close before 3,4 push; stack still empty at the close
addq(2, 1) = 3 (fully resolved before end); end is harmless
all-prefix form fully resolved before end
stack=[3,4]; addq fwd-collects 2; end forces close with fwd=[2] and stack-top=4 → sig=[2,4] → 4+2=6; the deeper 3 stays as residue. Result: "3 6".

leading end has no pending fwd → no-op; then 1 pushes; addq fwd-collects 2 (sig[0]); 3 fails — wait, 3 IS Integer. Actually addq fwd-collects 2 and 3 → sig=[2,3] → 3+2=5; stack=[1] residue. Result: "1 5".

column1: # def + fn build user functions. Bodies use the same numeric ops.
def inc ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n 1 addq ] ) 5 inc
column3: user fn computing n + 1 via addq inside the body

column1: def doub ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n n addq ] ) 5 doub
def sqr ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n n mulq ] ) 7 sqr
column3: user fn computing n * n via mulq

column1: def neg2 ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n negq ] ) 5 neg2
# Decimal version — the body's args/return types still match Integer
# in this fn (the spec runner's fn sig only accepts a single typed
# param list); but the Number-typed addq still handles Integer args
# normally. For Decimal args, the call would fail sig match at fn's
# [Integer] entry — pinned in the next row.
def inc ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n 1 addq ] ) 5.0 inc
# Composition: a user fn calling another user fn.
def inc ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n 1 addq ] ) def inc2 ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n inc inc ] ) 5 inc2
# Custom fn body using subq — exercises the b-a convention.
def diff ( fn [ a:Integer b:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ subq a b ] ) 10 diff 3
column3: diff(10, 3) → param a=3 (fwd), b=10 (stk); body subq a b → subq 3 10 → sig=[3, 10] → 10-3 = 7

column1: # List-body splice — same machinery, no fn wrap. Body splices
# in-place at the use site (see code-splice.tsv).
def trip ( fn [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n n addq n addq ] ) 4 trip
column3: body computes n + n + n via two chained addq calls
